news_communities.title: City of Boston and Kraft Group reach agreement on Everett soccer stadium
news_communities.author: Matt Prichard and Sue O'Connell
news_communities.summary:
A soccer stadium in Everett, Massachusetts, is one step closer to becoming a reality.

The Kraft Group has been working toward building a new home for the New England Revolution – a 25,000-seat stadium on the Mystic River. The project has passed state legislative hurdles but multiple communities raised concerns about the potential impact on local infrastructure, public transit and quality of life. There was a Dec. 31 deadline to make community mitigation agreements.

The Kraft Group and the City of Boston announced a community benefits package on Wednesday that would total $48 million over the next 15 years.

Mayor Michelle Wu’s office said the Kraft Group will make “substantial infrastructure improvements” for Sullivan Square in Charlestown prior to the stadium’s opening.

NBC10 Boston asked Wu about the agreement at First Night Boston.

“I am very pleased that we have reached a fair deal for the city of Boston, particularly for the residents of Charlestown and all of the many, many community leaders, neighborhood leaders, who have been part of making sure that there would be mitigation addressed,” she said. “This is a huge opportunity for the entire region, to bring jobs here, to make sure that we’re continuing to be the sports capital of the entire region.”

The Krafts will also fund all public safety and transportation management costs for stadium events and the city of Boston will collect a permanent per-ticket revenue source, totally $34 million over the first 15 years.

The Kraft Group has also reached an agreement with the City of Everett through negotiations with Mayor Carlo DeMaria, who has been supportive of the project.

“These agreements reflect years of collaboration with city leaders and community stakeholders to ensure the project delivers meaningful, lasting benefits for residents while addressing transportation, environmental, and municipal impacts. We are especially grateful for Mayor Carlo DeMaria’s leadership and vision. At its core, this is an investment in Everett and its future,” the company said in a statement.

“I know there are many more steps to come, but this is an important milestone that we’ve reached, and I’m very happy that we’re here,” Wu added.

news_communities.title: Medford firefighter arrested after child exploitation investigation
news_communities.author: Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts firefighter was arrested Monday night on charges of disseminating harmful material to minors.

The Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office said it learned this month that 63-year-old Hugh McEleney, a firefighter in Medford, was identified in a federal child exploitation investigation.

McEleney is accused of “communicating with and sending images to an individual he believed to be a juvenile female under 15-years-old,” according to the district attorney’s office.

At his arraignment Tuesday in Somerville District Court, prosecutors said the allegations stem from an interaction with an undercover police officer in St. Petersburg, Florida. McEleney is accused of sending nude images and videos of himself to the officer, who claimed to be 14 years old.

While state prosecutors said a federal task force investigated McEleney, they did not say whether federal charges were expected.

The commonwealth requested $20,000 cash bail with conditions that McEleney have no unsupervised contact with anyone under 18, not work or volunteer with anyone under 18, and have no internet or social media access.

The judge set bail at $1,000, granting the conditions requested by prosecutors, but doing so without prejudice, noting that he would consider modifying them after McEleney’s court-appointed attorney argued that the language was overly broad.

news_communities.title: Lynn ice rescue highlights common winter danger
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A person fell through the ice at a park in Lynn, Massachusetts, on Wednesday morning and had to be rescued by first responders.

Lynn police said officers were called to Flax Pond at Fecteau Park around 7:30 a.m. The victim was pulled from the water and taken to the hospital in stable condition.

A similar rescue unfolded in Malden, where a young girl fell through the ice at Fellsmere Pond.

Fire officials warned the public to stay off of the ice unless absolutely certain it is thick enough to support the weight.

Ice should be at least 4 inches thick for activities on foot like ice fishing, at least 5 inches for snowmobiles or ATVs, at least 8 to 12 inches for cars or small trucks, and at least 12 to 15 inches for medium trucks, according to the Massachusetts Division of Fisheries and Wildlife.

Moving bodies of water, like creeks and rivers, have higher risk for thin patches.

news_communities.title: Pedestrian injured in East Boston hit-and-run
news_communities.summary:

The crash happened on Saratoga Street. Police responded around 9:15 p.m.

The victim’s injuries are believed to be non-life-threatening.

Authorities did not give a description of the vehicle.

The crash is under investigation.

news_communities.title: Girl rescued after falling through the ice in Malden
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
Firefighters rescued a teen girl who had fallen through the ice into the frigid water of a pond in Malden, Massachusetts, on Wednesday afternoon.

It happened around 2 p.m. at Fellsmere Pond, fire officials said. Firefighters in water rescue suits went in to find the girl holding onto the edge of the ice, according to the Malden Fire Department. She was pulled from the water in under 30 seconds.

“I don’t even think the engine stopped,” Malden Fire Captain Roy Tribble said. “I jumped up, I sprinted down here, pretty much just jumped right into the water.”

The 14-year-old girl was conscious and alert when she was brought to a Boston hospital for treatment.

Tribble said the water was about 30 feet deep.

“A life was saved. It was a young girl who fell through the ice,” added Captain Bob Hegarty.

He warned everyone to stay off thin ice.

“A warning to everybody out there that ice, if it’s not thick enough, you’re going to fall through, and if you fall through, you’re not going to be able to get yourself out,” Hegarty said. “So stay off the ice until it’s very thick, which — who can tell when it’s very thick?”

news_communities.title: Man struck by car in Chelsea has critical injuries, police say
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A man hit by a car in Chelsea, Massachusetts, on Tuesday was rushed to the hospital with critical injuries, according to local police.

The man, a 33-year-old Malden resident, was hit around 5 p.m. on Second Street, across from the Market Basket entrance. He is in critical but stable condition at Mass General Hospital, according to police. His name has not been released.

The driver of the vehicle remained at the scene and cooperated with police, and was also taken to the hospital for evaluation.

Massachusetts State Police and the Chelsea Police Department are investigating. At this time, police said there are no charges pending against the driver, as it appears to be accidental due to heavy rain and poor visibility.

news_communities.title: Man shot dead in East Boston
news_communities.summary:
A man was killed in a shooting in East Boston on Tuesday night.

Boston police confirmed they were called to the area of 27 Havre Street around 5:50 p.m. where they found the victim suffering from a gunshot wound. He was taken to the hospital, where he was pronounced dead.

He was identified as 21-year-old Nazir Wallace, a Cambridge resident.

No arrests have been made and an investigation is underway.

More details were not immediately available.

news_communities.title: Persons of interest ID'd in attack on woman and children outside Square One Mall
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
Police have identified two persons of interest in an attack on a woman and her three children outside the Square One Mall in Saugus, Massachusetts, last week, according to local police.

The victim told police that she was leaving the mall with her three children around 8:30 p.m on Nov. 24 when they were confronted by two men who had broken into their car.

One of the men showed a knife and the other, according to police, punched an 11-year-old in the stomach before running off.

Police released a surveillance photo showing two people of interest in the attack last week. On Monday they confirmed they have identified those people. They are juveniles and will not be publicly named, police said.

news_communities.title: Woman and children attacked outside Square One Mall in Saugus
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A woman and children were attacked by two people who broke into their vehicle at the Square One Mall in Saugus, Massachusetts, on Monday night, according to local police.

The victim told police that she was leaving the mall with her three children around 8:30 p.m. after a shopping trip when they were confronted by two men who had broken into their car.

One of the men showed a knife and the other, according to police, punched an 11-year-old in the stomach before running off.

The victim said she got into the car and drove to somewhere she felt safe before calling 911.

The people being sought are described as men between 18 and 20 years old, wearing all black, including beanie-style hats and masks. Police initially described them as suspects, but later referred to them as “persons of interest.”

Police, who called the assault “serious act of violence,” are looking for witnesses who they believe may have seen the incident.

Investigators believe the people being sought ran off toward the bus stop area of the mall.

Anyone with information is urged to call police.

news_communities.title: NH man faces charges in 1991 rape in Peabody, Mass.
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
A New Hampshire man has been charged 34 years after what prosecutors described as a brutal rape in Peabody, Massachusetts.

Darrell Lavoie, of Nashua, was ordered held on $100,000 bail over the Nov. 12, 1991, sexual assault, the Essex County District Attorney’s Office said Monday, after he faced charges including aggravated rape, assault and battery with a dangerous weapon and breaking and entering in Essex Superior Court.

The woman was returning to her house from her job when she was blindfolded and raped by a person holding a knife, prosecutors said.

“The victim has been looking over her shoulder for 34 years, unable to put a face to the person who brutally raped her in her own home,” Assistant District Attorney Jessica Strasnick said in court.

Strasnick told the judge that a girlfriend of Lavoie’s who knew the woman had previously shared a suspicion that he was involved, but police hadn’t been able to connect him to the case.

Nearly two decades ago, a DNA profile of the attacker was created, but Lavoie wasn’t identified as the suspect until Massachusetts State Police investigators took a new look at the case earlier this year and obtained court order to test the man this summer, leading to the charges against him, prosecutors said.

Lavoie’s lawyer said he’s seen no convictions on the 59-year-old’s record, and that he’s a decorated combat veteran who served in Iraq. He asked for $7,500 bail, while prosecutors sought $250,000.

He’s due back in court Jan. 9.

news_communities.title: In building a new soccer stadium, new team in RI could have lessons for the Revs
news_communities.author: Glenn Jones
news_communities.summary:
The city of Boston and the Kraft Group have weeks to agree on a mitigation plan for the proposed New England Revolution soccer stadium in Everett. If they cannot agree by Dec. 31, state law requires an arbitrator to step in and make decisions for them.

Boston Mayor Michelle Wu has publicly blasted the Kraft Group’s offer for managing issues like traffic and environmental concerns as too small. “Unserious,” was her description at a recent news conference.

Dueling press statements that underscore contentiousness between sides were also common in the lead-up to the opening of another New England soccer-specific stadium, the newly built home of Rhode Island Football Club in Pawtucket, Rhode Island.

That stadium’s cost ballooned to $130 million, said to be the costliest minor league soccer stadium in the country. About 20% of that bill, around $27 million, is covered with public funds.

In the proposal stage, the project was unpopular with the state’s residents, but today, just after RIFC completed its first season there, fans raved about Centerville Bank Stadium.

“I wholeheartedly do not support a soccer stadium funded by taxpayer dollars,” Merrill Sampson, a season ticketholder from Bristol, told NBC10 Boston at the final home game of the regular season. “But you know, decisions were made. The team was here. So my reaction was: I want to support the team. I want the team to do well.”

Neighbors with homes just a few hundred yards from the stadium opposed the project in the early days, but after 18 RIFC home games and a slew of other events, it was difficult to find anyone who felt the same way they did two years ago.

“It’s about community,” RIFC President David Peart said about the team’s role in shifting attitudes. “It’s about being involved and engaged with the community groups and organizations throughout the region. That’s really important to us and we’re committed to that.”

The mayor of Everett, who must also sign a mitigation agreement with the Kraft Group ahead of that proposed stadium’s development, believes Boston is standing in the way of a bright future on the Mystic River waterfront for his city.

“I’ve tried to reach out to the mayor of Boston a few times. Now that her election is over, I think we’ll be able to collaborate real soon,” Mayor Carlo DeMaria said. “She’s a huge transit advocate, especially with buses and bike lanes, and I am as well. So I think we align greatly on that.”

It could cost more than $500 million to transform the former energy plant into a waterfront soccer stadium and park. The Kraft Group is proposing to do it with no taxpayer funds.

news_communities.title: Purple Heart found in forgotten deposit box returned to Mass. veteran
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts Vietnam War veteran had a Purple Heart medal returned to him through the state’s Unclaimed Property Division, officials said Monday.

The Purple Heart belonging to Everett resident James Mooney, who spent over a year serving with the Army in Vietnam was found in a forgotten safety deposit box, according to the office of Treasurer Deborah Goldberg.

Three other Purple Hearts are being held by the state’s Unclaimed Property Division — two more have been returned, officials said, part of an effort to return lost military honors to veterans and their families.

“Returning this Purple Heart is about more than reuniting someone with a lost possession, it is about honoring the service and sacrifice of one of our nation’s heroes,” Goldberg said in a statement about the ceremony for Mooney. “A Purple Heart is an honor that represents courage, dedication, and an unwavering commitment to put other’s lives ahead of one’s own, all while protecting our country’s freedom. We are incredibly honored to be able to return this medal to Mr. Mooney.”

Goldberg’s office said they work to return objects of personal significance to their rightful owner or their heirs.

news_communities.title: Cava opens new restaurant in Boston suburbs
news_communities.author: Boston Restaurant Talk
news_communities.summary:
[This story first appeared on Boston Restaurant Talk.]

A Washington, D.C.-based chain of fast-casual Mediterranean spots has opened a new location in the northern suburbs of Boston.

According to a press release, Cava is now open at MarketStreet Lynnfield, and like its other locations, it offers such options as bowls, pitas, salads, and more. The new outlet joins a number of others in the Greater Boston area, including four outlets north of the city in Beverly, Chelmsford, North Andover and Woburn.

The address for the new location of Cava at MarketStreet Lynnfield is 335 Market Street, Lynnfield, MA, 01940. The website for all locations can be found at cava.com.

(Follow Marc on Bluesky at @marchurboston.bsky.social)

news_communities.title: Man arrested in Halloween assault on Lynn convenience store clerk
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
A man has been arrested in an attack that injured a convenience store clerk on Halloween night in Lynn, Massachusetts, police said Friday.

Joshua Mora, 22, was arrested on Friday with the help of state police on charges of armed robbery, assault and battery and malicious destruction of property, Lynn police said.

The assault at the Richdale Convenience Store on Chatham Street was reported as a fight about 7:21 p.m. on Halloween, police said. Items were stolen and the clerk was severely attacked, for which he was sent to the hospital.

Video of the assault shared with NBC10 Boston showed a person in a hooded sweatshirt go over the counter and beat the clerk. Police said the worker had significant injuries.

Police didn’t say how Mora was identified as the suspect or where he was arrested.

Mora is due to be arraigned Friday. It wasn’t immediately clear if he had an attorney who could speak to the charges.

news_communities.title: Boyfriend sentenced in murder of Woburn woman found in Saugus marsh
news_communities.author: Staff Reports
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts man was sentenced to life in prison without parole nearly four years after his girlfriend’s body was found in a Saugus marsh.

Jurors convicted Bruce Maiben, a 48-year-old Lynn resident, of first-degree murder on Monday.

Sherell Pringle, a 40-year-old Woburn woman, was reported missing after she did not return home from a date with Maiben on Dec. 18, 2021. Family and friends found her body two days later in the Rumney Marsh Reservation off of Route 107 in Saugus.

Pringle’s mother, Pearl Garner, gave a victim impact statement at Maiben’s sentencing hearing Wednesday.

“When Sherell was murdered, a piece of me died with her,” Garner said through tears. “I think about her all the time and want to know why — why you did something like this, so vicious.”

Garner said Maiben stabbed her daughter more than 200 times, leading to a closed-casket funeral.

After Pringle was found, Maiben was arrested on charges of larceny over $1,200, tampering with evidence and obstruction of justice. Prosecutors later charged him with murder.

Family and friends of Pringle have said they used technology to find out where her phone was — a catch basin on the side of the road — and backtracked from there. Then they saw a purse by the road.

“What hurts the most is, I told these cops from the jump to locate Bruce Maiben,” Pringle’s son, Jahmani Larionne, said at the time. “I told them Day 1, ‘You need to located Bruce Maiben. He lives in Lynn. You need to ping his phone and find him.'”

news_communities.title: No injuries as Sky Ranger makes emergency landing in Chelsea
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
Sky Ranger, NBC10 Boston’s news helicopter, had to make an emergency landing in Chelsea, Massachusetts, on Tuesday afternoon.

NBC10 Boston can confirm our helicopter needed to land due to a problem with the aircraft. The crew landed safely and there are no injuries.

Chelsea police confirmed they observed the chopper in the sky before it landed in an open area on Marginal Street

“I was observing it and something didn’t look right, it was teetering a little bit and there was a little bit I observed of what I appeared to be a little bit of smoke coming out of the tail section of it and then I saw it go down,” explained Sgt. Jose Otero.

Fire Chief John Quatieri added that firefighters responded and found no signs of fire when they arrived.

First responders praised the pilot for finding an open space in the densely populated community to safely land.

news_communities.title: Driver arrested after crashing stolen vehicle in Peabody, police say
news_communities.summary:
Authorities arrested a Massachusetts man accused of crashing a stolen vehicle after fleeing from police in Peabody.

Police tried to stop the driver on Lynn Street, but he turned around and fled, according to the Peabody Police Department. Officers ended the chase because of the driver’s high speed.

A short time later, the driver hit two other vehicles, causing non-life-threatening injuries. Police did not say how many people were hurt.

The driver then lost control, going off the road and into the yard of a home, damaging the property and nearly hitting the garage, police said.

Authorities arrested 32-year-old Timothy Crane of Danvers on charges of receiving stolen property, failure to stop for police, operating to endanger and leaving the scene of a crash causing personal injury.

news_communities.title: Tree falls on 2 homes in Malden
news_communities.summary:
A large tree came crashing down overnight near several homes in Malden, Massachusetts.

Malden police and fire departments were on Prentiss Street early Thursday morning, where the tree appeared to have caused minor damage to two homes.

There was no immediate word on any injuries.

Pictures from the scene showed the tree split in half, falling into the front yards.

news_communities.title: Caught on video: Man charged in attack on man wearing inflatable Trump costume
news_communities.author: Erin Logan
news_communities.summary:
A man is facing criminal charges after allegedly attacking a person in an inflatable costume of President Donald Trump in Swampscott, Massachusetts.

Jonathan Silveira, a Trump supporter from Peabody, said he was walking to an anti-Trump “No Kings” rally when he was attacked near King’s Beach. He said he didn’t make it to the protest.

“He just didn’t want me there. He kept yelling and screaming,” Silveira said. “I kept telling him to get away from me, and he kept telling people around him that he wanted to punch me in the face.”

The incident was captured on video by Silveira’s girlfriend, as was the arrest of the suspect, Michael Curll.

“I don’t understand why he got that crazy. I was just trying to get a few laughs,” Silveira said. “I thought it would be a couple of back-and-forths, you know, nothing like that.”

In court Monday, Curll pleaded not guilty to assault and battery with a dangerous weapon.

His attorney argued that Silveira started the altercation, claiming his client was hit in the face with a metal pole.

Silveira denied that account of the events.

“Absolutely not,” he told NBC10 Boston. “Not even close.”

Curll is due back in court in December. He is facing several unrelated charges in Massachusetts and Connecticut, including assault and battery, drug possession and criminal trespassing.

news_communities.title: Immigration attorney sues Trump administration after phone seized at Logan
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain
news_communities.summary:
An immigration attorney in Chelsea, Massachusetts, is suing the federal government after agents with the Department of Homeland Security seized his cellphone at Logan Airport in Boston — an action he said was unreasonable and a violation of his clients’ rights.

Andrew Lattarulo has represented several high-profile clients in Massachusetts, including a 13-year-old boy recently detained in Everett by Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents and a Brazilian woman in Worcester whose detainment in May prompted outrage by immigrant advocates. He filed a suit asking a federal judge to order the Trump administration to destroy the data it took from his phone.

“Attorney Lattarulo brought this case to protect his clients,” said Patrick Hanley, Lattarulo’s attorney. “He’s very aware and very knowledgeable of the administration’s current enforcement priorities. He makes the people that are interested in that, like his clients, aware of it.”

According to court filings, Lattarulo was returning from a trip to Aruba with his brother and his law partner, Audai Cote, on Sept. 27 when federal agents approached him at Terminal C and confiscated his phone without a warrant.

The government argued in court that agents had “reasonable suspicion,” noting that Homeland Security Investigations had been looking into Lattarulo’s law firm for possibly employing undocumented workers.

Investigators said they flagged 117 people in the firm’s employment verification forms — also known as I-9s — who allegedly lacked proper work documentation.

Lattarulo’s lawyers said that only his phone was seized, not his partner’s, and that the government is retaliating against him for publicly criticizing federal immigration policies online.

“There does seem to be another motive, and we don’t see the government arguing that they’re looking for information on account of him being an immigration attorney,” said Stefanie Fisher-Pinkert, an immigration lawyer who liaises with Customs and Border Protection at Logan Airport. “In our liaison conversations with CBP, they’ve noted that there is training for officers, and if somebody says, ‘Hey, I’m an attorney, and I have attorney-client privileged information on there,’ that they are required to take certain precautions. That doesn’t mean they don’t have that reasonable suspicion on an unrelated topic.”

Other immigration attorneys, including Eloa Celedon, expressed concern about what this means for their profession.

“We’re walking around like, ‘Are we targets, or is my phone going to be taken away, is my laptop going to be taken away?'” Celedon said.

“On rare occasions, officers/agents may search a traveler’s mobile phone, or other electronic devices during the inspection process,” the Department of Homeland Security said in a statement. “CBP has established strict guidelines to ensure that these searches are exercised judiciously and responsibly.”

For now, a federal judge has ordered the government not to search or destroy the copied data from Lattarulo’s phone until she makes a final ruling.

news_communities.title: High-speed NH chase ends with crash, killing Mass. driver, police say
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts man died when his car crashed off a New Hampshire highway following a high-speed chase early Sunday morning, police said.

Benjamin Tapia Gately reached 120 mph while being pursued by New Hampshire State Police, who’d tried to pull his speeding car over on Interstate 93 in the Concord area about 12:51 a.m., the agency said.

The car didn’t stop until Tapia Gately tried to take an off-ramp at Exit 18, in Canterbury, and lost control, traveling down an embankment and hitting a tree, according to police.

Tapia Gately, a 20-year-old from Saugus, was thrown from the car and pronounced dead at the scene, despite first responders attempting to save his life, police said. No one else was in the car.

Police were investigating what happened, and anyone with information about the incident was urge to contact Sgt. Kenneth McGrath at 603-223-4381 or Kenneth.T.McGrath@dos.nh.gov.

news_communities.title: Malden woman accused of threatening federal agents making immigration arrest
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts woman is accused of threatening to kill federal agents who were conducting immigration enforcement in Malden.

Bethany Abigail Terrill, 37, was charged with threatening a United States official, the U.S. Attorney’s Office said Thursday.

Prosecutors say that Terrill approached the agents as they were making an arrest outside Malden District Court on Sept. 29, describing her as verbally abusive. She allegedly shouted things like “ICE is here, ICE is here,” “You guys are monsters, this is insane,” “Sir, what’s your name, what’s your name,” “I can try to help you,” and “I am an American civilian, I have a right to be here,” and called the agents Nazis.

She is further accused of trying to push past the agents, yelling “Charlie Kirk died, and we love it… We’re coming for you, gonna kill you.” 

According to the statement of probable cause, an agent heard the threat and proceeded to detain Terrill, who they said was “actively resistant and fought to escape.”

Officials say the agents were wearing body cameras, and Terrill was also filming at the time. In the statement of probable cause, investigators say the threat can be heard in the footage from Terrill’s phone.

The agents on scene were from multiple agencies, including Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), ICE and the FBI.

During her appearance in U.S. District Court in Boston on Thursday, a public defender said Terrill owns a salon and was struggling with legal issues in an unrelated case.

Judge Paul Levenson ordered Terrill be released with conditions, saying “federal agents have a right to not be subjected to threats.”

Terrill was ordered as part of her conditions of release to participate in a mental health program for treatment on a regular basis, to develop a safety plan for her own safety and others, avoid contact with potential witnesses, have no contact with any federal official, family or otherwise in any way or form, refrain from alcohol and the use of any illegal drugs, to avoid contact with law enforcement, and to refrain from making any further threats.

She didn’t speak to reporters outside of court.

The charge of threatening a U.S. official carries a sentence of up to 10 years in prison, three years of supervised release and a fine of $250,000.

news_communities.title: New details on emergency work on bridge connecting Saugus and Lynn
news_communities.author: Malcolm Johnson
news_communities.summary:
A bridge connecting the eastern Massachusetts communities of Saugus and Lynn will be closed until further notice.

The Massachusetts Department of Transportation announced the closure of the Route 107 bridge for repair work on Wednesday. Pedestrians and cyclists can continue to use the sidewalks of the bridge.

Saugus police said the closure was required due to “findings from a recent bridge inspection.” The department explained that detours would be in effect.

MassDOT said Thursday that inspectors found cracking on bridge deck panel assemblies. Crews are working to make repairs, which will take days. The bridge is expected to reopen by the end of the weekend.

People in the area told NBC10 Boston Wednesday that they are concerned about traffic impacts.

April Young, a bartender at the Fox Hill Yacht Club, says she will have to leave home earlier to make it to work on time.

“At least like 20 minutes earlier than I usually do, cause I’m going to have to cut through Lynn, down Boston Street, and then Lincoln Ave.,” she said. “Everybody’s going to be doing that, so it is going to suck.”

The current bridge is part of a bridge replacement project. More changes are expected in the area later this fall, when the new bridge opens to traffic. At first, according to MassDOT, cars will be restricted to one lane in each direction so crews can remove the temporary bridge.Long term, the goal is to have a bridge with two travel lanes, bike lines and sidewalks in both directions by the summer of 2026.

news_communities.title: New expected end date for emergency closure of bridge connecting Saugus, Lynn
news_communities.author: Amelia Fabiano and Jeff Saperstone
news_communities.summary:
The Route 107 bridge between Lynn and Saugus, Massachusetts, is expected to remain closed for emergency repairs for several more days.

The bridge used by thousands of drivers a day was shut down Wednesday evening after engineers discovered a crack in one of the metal plates that cars and trucks drive on. The plate is structural in nature, according to the Massachusetts Department of Transportation.

Agency officials said Thursday that they’re hoping the bridge reopens by the end of the weekend.

The detours around the bridge have caused massive delays for drivers, especially during the morning and evening commutes.

The bridge is a temporary overpass due to ongoing work on the Belden G. Bly Bridge next to it. MassDOT says northbound traffic should follow the detour at Ballard Street, while southbound traffic should take the alternate route on Summer Street and follow signs.

“There will be a lot of traffic coming out of Revere, coming into Saugus and into Lynn. I mean that’s a lifeline into Lynn from 107 going in there,” said Robert Schoen, who uses the bridge.

“It takes one hour to make you to come around, you know? To go down Summer Street, come through Lynn, come back through Saugus, takes one hour,” another man said. “Torture.”

Business in the area said they expect customer traffic to dip. Jack’s Pizza co-owner Nilan Patell found the impact shocking.

“I know it’s a hard commute, but I hope people can still come out and enjoy their lunch. Dinner, snack on their way home. That’s all I can hope,” he said.

The bridge’s drawbridge function has remained operational so boats can still pass underneath. Walkers, runners and bicyclists can also still use the sidewalks.

news_communities.title: Man shot in vehicle in East Boston, police say
news_communities.author: Jericho Tran and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
A man was critically injured in a shooting Wednesday night in East Boston, according to authorities.

Boston police say the shooting happened around 7:57 p.m. in the area of 321 Brandywyne Drive. The victim, an adult male whose name has not been released, was in a vehicle at the time. He was taken to a local hospital with life-threatening injuries.

Aerial footage from the scene showed a large police response near a residential building. A vehicle could be seen up against a container, blocked off by tape.

The shooting happened in the area of Brandywyne Village, a private housing complex.

“Police are actively investigating a shooting at or near Brandywyne Village,” a management spokesperson for the complex said in a statement. “We are fully cooperating with the police and there is no active threat to the community at this time. We will keep our residents informed.”

No arrests have been made, according to police, who said homicide detectives responded to the scene. Further details have not been released.

An investigation is active and ongoing.

news_communities.title: Location of Shaq's chicken restaurant closes in move from 1 Mass. mall to another
news_communities.author: Boston Restaurant Talk
news_communities.summary:
[This story first appeared on Boston Restaurant Talk.]

A chain of chicken spots founded by an NBA hall of famer has closed a location in the Greater Boston area after a bit more than one year in business, though it will be reborn in a new location not too far away.

According to a source, Big Chicken at the Northshore Mall in Peabody is no longer in operation, with a Patch article confirming that it has indeed shut down. We have subsequently been informed that this is actually a move, with a new location of the restaurant coming to 19 Dodge Street in Beverly, possibly early in 2026.

The dining spot, which is part of a chain founded by Shaquille O’Neal, first opened in the summer of 2024, offering such items as chicken sandwiches, popcorn chicken, sliders, tenders, macaroni and cheese, and more. A food truck location of the chain (called Big Chicken Dunk Truck) remains open at the Wrentham Village Premium Outlets.

Big Chicken was first established in 2018 by O’Neal, who played for the Orlando Magic, Los Angeles Lakers, Miami Heat, Phoenix Suns, and Cleveland Cavaliers before finishing his career with the Boston Celtics in 2011. Its website can be found at bigchicken.com.

(Follow Marc on Bluesky at @marchurboston.bsky.social)

news_communities.title: Cities bordering Everett unite in Revolution soccer stadium transparency push
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
Four cities that border Everett, Massachusetts, have banded together in a push for more involvement in the New England Revolution’s plans for a soccer-specific stadium in the city.

The mayors of Revere, Medford, Malden and Chelsea formed a coalition called City Mayors for Citizens that they said will seek transparency on plans for the stadium, including the impacts it will have on their communities.

The 25,000-seat stadium proposal has progressed in the state Legislature but still faces plenty of hurdles. The City Mayors for Citizens said they are worried about how the building on along the Mystic River might impact local infrastructure, public transit and quality of life.

“A project of this size and scale will have significant regional impacts,” said Revere Mayor Patrick Keefe Jr said in a statement. “We want to ensure that a stadium is contributing positively to the communities it is drawing resources from. Our residents should not bear the burdens of a private stadium that has strong potential to negatively affect their day-to-day lives.”

Asked for a response to the statement, a Revolution club spokesperson noted that it expects to work with neighboring communities after it reaches agreements with Everett and Boston, which would allow the property to have its port designation removed and the process of construction to continue.

The spokesperson also noted that the team thanked the mayors and promised to review the concerns raised in their letter, sent Monday.

news_communities.title: Body of teen killed in Swampscott in 1974 exhumed in new search for evidence
news_communities.author: Mary Markos, Kaitlin McKinley Becker and Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
The body of a 15-year-old killed over half a century ago was exhumed Wednesday at a cemetery in Swampscott, Massachusetts, as investigators search for more evidence in the unsolved case, officials said.

Henry Bedard Jr., vanished on Dec. 16, 1974, and his body was found under a pile of leaves by a 10-year-old boy the next day. Nearby was a baseball bat inscribed with unique markings. Investigators have spent decades chasing leads, conducting interviews and analyzing evidence.

Essex County District Attorney Paul Tucker confirmed Wednesday afternoon that a large search at a cemetery on Essex Street was connected with the ongoing investigation into Henry’s death.

“Advances in forensic science and technology have led to new criminal investigative avenues that were not possible in the past,” he said in a statement. “We are hopeful that these advances will lead to significant evidence.”

The teenager’s body was exhumed under a court order requested after investigators recently received information, according to Tucker’s office — they didn’t specify what that information was.

Prosecutors and police urged anyone with information about who killed Henry to contact investigators at 1-855-MA-SOLVE or mspunresolved@pol.state.ma.us.

Officials noted that Henry was remembered as a baseball and football player beloved in the town. He worked at his father’s Danvers service station.

“What happened to Henry has never left this town, and young or old, all of the community still talks about Henry and what happened on that fateful day,” Swampscott Police Chief Ruben Quesada said in a statement.

The body was exhumed — taken out of the ground — and taken to Boston for a post-mortem autopsy, Tucker said earlier in a brief update at the cemetery.

A large number of police and other investigators were seen at the cemetery. Two tents — one from the Swampscott Police Department and one from the DA’s office — were shielding an area around a few headstones, where people could be seen digging in one plot with shovels. An excavator was assisting.

A large group of people were seen gathered around the tents, where it appeared police were exhuming a body.

news_communities.title: Man accused of choking child, 5, in Mass. arrested by U.S. Marshals in Maine
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
A man wanted for allegedly choking a 5-year-old child in Massachusetts, then fleeing in the mother’s vehicle, was arrested Tuesday in Maine, the U.S. Marshals said.

Dominque Bailey was taken into custody in Lewiston, Maine, when federal, state and local law enforcement forced their way into a residence, the Marshals said Wednesday.

Bailey was wanted on strangulation or suffocation and domestic assault and battery charges over the incident in Peabody, Massachusetts, according to the Marshals.

If you or someone you know is experiencing domestic violence, contact the National Domestic Violence Hotline by calling 1-800-799-SAFE (7233), visiting www.thehotline.org or texting LOVEIS to 22522.

They didn’t say when the child was choked in Peabody or what the child’s condition was, but did say the vehicle Bailey allegedly stole was found in Maine several weeks ago. That led investigators to look into known associates of Bailey’s.

He was charged as a fugitive in Maine, where he was also on probation, which he’s set to be charged with violating, and was due for an extradition hearing to clear the way for him to return and face the charges in Massachusetts.

It wasn’t immediately clear if he had an attorney who could speak to the charges.

news_communities.title: Peabody educator returning to class after Charlie Kirk comment suspension
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
An educator who’d been on leave for allegedly violating the school social media policy for Peabody, Massachusetts, in the wake of Charlie Kirk’s killing is set to return to the classroom this week, the district and its teachers union announced Tuesday.

Multiple Massachusetts teachers were placed on leave over comments allegedly made online about Kirk’s death, including two in Peabody. Superintendent Josh Vadala said at the time that the investigation into the two high school teachers concerned alleged policy violations “including but not limited to inappropriate social media posts following the death of Charlie Kirk,” something they took seriously.

On Tuesday, Peabody Public Schools and the Peabody Federation of Teachers together announced that an educator would be returning to the classroom following a review of the allegations.

“It is important that our community work together to foster greater understanding and respect opinions that differ from our own. We hope that the same things students are learning in the classroom, including how to engage in debate thoughtfully, the importance of empathy, and how to be a critical thinker, can find their way back into our public discourse,” the statement said.

They didn’t identify the educator, their role or the school they work at.

Teachers union AFT Massachusetts discussed the agreement in a statement posted on social media thanking the district and Peabody Mayor Ted Bettencourt for working with them “to ensure that due process is upheld and the needs of students are put first.”

news_communities.title: 12-year-old identified as suspect in two fires at Marblehead church
news_communities.author: Marc Fortier
news_communities.summary:
A 12-year-old has been identified as the sole suspect in two recent fires at a church in Marblehead, Massachusetts.

Marblehead police and fire, in conjunction with the State Fire Marshal’s Office, said they are investigating two fires at Our Lady, Star of the Sea, a Catholic church on Atlantic Avenue.

Around 11:42 a.m. on Sunday, police and firefighters responded to a small fire in the church’s bathroom. They said a similar incident occurred a week earlier, on Sept. 21. In each case, the small fires were quickly extinguished, causing no permanent damage.

The results of an investigation into both fires determined that the fires were intentionally set, and fire and police officials said an unnamed 12-year-old has been identified as the sole suspect. They did not say what, if any, charges the suspect will face.

“We take all fire incidents seriously, no matter how small, and are grateful for the teamwork that helped us bring this case to a swift resolution,” Marblehead Fire Chief Jason Gilliland said. “We are thankful to the parish staff for their vigilance, and to our partners in law enforcement who assisted in this investigation.”

“These incidents appear to be isolated, and there is no identifiable threat to the community or the parishioners at Star of the Sea Church,” Marblehead Police Chief Dennis King added. “We are ever vigilant to protect our places of worship and appreciate the assistance of our public safety partners and the church staff to identify the individual responsible and hold them accountable.”

Authorities in nearby Beverly are also investigating a fire overnight at a church there, but the State Fire Marshal’s Office said in a statement Monday that there is no connection to the fires in Marblehead.

“State Police fire investigators assigned to the State Fire Marshal’s office assisted Marblehead officials with the investigation into yesterday’s fire in a church bathroom and a similar fire on 9/21/25. Both fires were small, caused minimal damage, and were quickly extinguished. A pre-teen juvenile took responsibility for both,” a spokesperson for the State Fire Marshal’s Office said in an email.

news_communities.title: Video shows Salvadoran man being detained by ICE in Malden
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain, Marc Fortier and Mark Garfinkel
news_communities.summary:
The wife of a Salvadoran man says he was violently taken into custody by immigration officials on his way to work in Malden, Massachusetts, on Wednesday morning.

The incident occurred shortly before 6 a.m. in the area of 28 Kennedy Drive.

Leslie Perlera Gonzalez said she received a distressing call from her husband, Edgar Hernan Elias Escobar, shortly before 6 a.m. as he was heading to his construction job. She rushed to Kennedy Drive, where she said Elias Escobar was surrounded by immigration officers.

“I heard them smash his window, and he was saying, ‘Ow, you’re hurting me,'” Perlera Gonzalez said. “When I arrived on the scene, he had a Taser on his face.”

The 31-year-old had no criminal record and was in the process of adjusting his immigration status after the couple married last year, noted Perlera Gonzalez.

“He’s never come close to doing anything that would be considered a crime,” she said.

She alleged that agents used an unmarked car to ram her husband’s vehicle, forcing him off the road before detaining him.

Malden police said Elias Escobar sustained minor injuries. He was transported on a stretcher to a hospital in Melrose, where Perlera Gonzalez said she was not allowed to see him.

About an hour later, he was moved to a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement processing facility in Burlington.

A spokesperson from the Department of Homeland Security said in a statement that Elias Escobar was the target of the ICE operation and suffered “no injuries.”

“On September 17, 2025, ICE conducted an immigration enforcement operation in Malden, Massachusetts to arrest Edgar Hernan Elias-Escobar, an illegal alien from El Salvador. During the arrest, the illegal alien refused to comply with officer commands to rolldown his window. The officers took appropriate action and followed their training to use the minimum amount of force necessary to resolve the situation in a manner that ensured the success of the operation and prioritized the safety of the public and our officers,” the spokesperson said in a statement. “Out of an abundance of caution, ICE law enforcement escorted Elias-Escobar to the hospital for evaluations. Medical staff found he had no injuries and discharged him. He was then escorted to the Boston Filed office for processing and will be placed in removal proceedings.”

“I’ve been expecting this from somebody I know every day since Donald Trump was elected,” said Amiee Parco, an attorney with Greater Boston Legal Services and a colleague of Perlera Gonzalez. “I’ve been waiting for it to affect me personally.”

Friends and coworkers gathered outside the Burlington facility, joining a weekly protest to support Elias Escobar’s release and that of other detainees.

“He’s a really wonderful person,” said Alyssa Rao, a friend of the couple. “I think this is going to impact everyone.”

After Wednesday’s ordeal, Perlera Gonzalez fears what her husband is going through while detained.

“I’m terrified of what could be happening when no one is watching,” she said.

At last check, Escobar was still at the facility. It’s unclear how long he’ll be held — often, people stay several days, though the facility is meant to detain people for hours.

His lawyer is trying to prevent the government from sending him to a far-away detention center, and from deportation.

ICE has ramped up arrests across greater Boston in recent weeks under “Operation Patriot 2.0,” detaining immigrants in public spaces.

Gov. Maura Healey spoke out against the sweeps on Tuesday, saying federal immigration enforcement officers are “creating an incredible amount of fear and intimidation.”

“Some of it seems purposeful, to be honest. Why else are you parked outside a church? Why are you parked outside a community center?”

The governor said her office has received no information from ICE about who is being detained or why.

“This is not a furtherance of public safety,” she said. “In fact, it undermines public safety.”

Asked what she can do to protest White House policy, Healey said: “I join with other governors in urging the Trump administration, in the strongest possible terms, to change course.”

State House News Service contributed to this report.

news_communities.title: New charges for teen driver in crash that killed girl in Marblehead
news_communities.author: Asher Klein, Marc Fortier and Jericho Tran
news_communities.summary:
A teenage driver has been arrested on new charges in the crash that killed a 13-year-old girl in Marblehead, Massachusetts, early Tuesday morning, officials said.

The 16-year-old faced charges of motor vehicle homicide by reckless operation, receiving a stolen vehicle and operating under the influence in Lynn Juvenile Court, the Essex County District Attorney’s Office said Wednesday. The teenager, whose name isn’t being shared because he’s under age, was initially only facing a charge of operating under the influence.

The driver is also facing two civil motor vehicle infractions, according to prosecutors. They didn’t share if a plea was entered on his behalf, or if he had a lawyer who could respond to the charges.

The crash killed Marblehead eighth-grader Savanah Gatchell, whose family was left devastated.

“There is an empty hole in my chest,” her mother, Christine Gatchell, told NBC10 Boston Tuesday night. “I don’t know how to move on.”

The crash took place on Atlantic Avenue just before 3 a.m., prosecutors have said. First responders found a vehicle extensively damaged, with a 13-year-old girl suffering from life-threatening injuries.

Marblehead Public Schools Superintendent John Robidoux said Tuesday that the girl had been set to start the eighth grade in days, and that grief counselors would be made available at Veterans Middle School and Marblehead High School.

In announcing the new charges on Wednesday, prosecutors initially gave a different spelling of Savanah’s name.

More on the deadly Marblehead crash

Girl, 13, dies in Marblehead crash, 16-year-old driver charged with OUI

13-year-old girl mourned after deadly Marblehead crash

news_communities.title: 13-year-old girl mourned after deadly Marblehead crash
news_communities.author: Jericho Tran, Marc Fortier and Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
What to Know

  • Savannah Gatchell, a 13-year-old girl was going into eighth grade at Marblehead Veterans Middle School, died in an early morning car crash that closed Atlantic Avenue in town Tuesday morning.
  • A 16-year-old boy believed to be driving the vehicle was charged with operating under the influence after being taken to a hospital with injuries.
  • The leaders of the Marblehead Public Schools and Marblehead Police Department shared statements with condolences.

A Massachusetts family is grieving after a 13-year-old girl was killed in a Marblehead crash.

A 16-year-old boy is charged with operating under the influence in the crash that killed Savannah Gatchell early Tuesday morning on Atlantic Avenue.

“She was always there,” her sister, Allysa Otero, said Tuesday. “Always loving.”

Family members say Gatchell was going to start eighth grade in a few days at Marblehead Veterans Middle School. They remember her as a loving sister and kind daughter.

A memorial to her rests on the broken wall where the car crashed.

“I just know that he picked her up, and I was in New York and I got the call,” Otero said.

“There is an empty hole in my chest,” said her mother, Christine Gatchell. “I don’t know how to move on.”

Marblehead Public Schools Superintendent John Robidoux said her death was “a devastating loss.”

“As we mourn with one another, we will support our students, faculty, and families with help from our administration and mental health teams,” Robidoux said.

Grief counselors will be available this week and into the school year at Veterans Middle School and Marblehead High School, he said, adding that the district was extending “heartfelt condolences to both families involved in this tragedy.”

Marblehead Police Chief Dennis King said the department was “extremely saddened” by the girl’s death.

“We share our deepest condolences with the victim’s family, loved ones, and anyone who is touched by this great loss. Our thoughts are with you,” King said in a statement. “Our priority in the coming days will be to support our community, and most importantly the victim’s family, as they navigate the loss of a daughter, family member, and friend.”

Marblehead police said in a social media post shortly after 6 a.m. that traffic on Atlantic Avenue was being rerouted due to the crash.

In an update Tuesday afternoon, the Essex District Attorney’s Office and Marblehead police said the crash occurred shortly before 3 a.m. in the area of 352 Atlantic Avenue.

Marblehead police and fire units responding to the scene found a vehicle with extensive damage. They said the girl suffered life-threatening injuries in the single-vehicle crash and died at the scene.

Authorities said a second person, a 16-year-old boy believed to be the driver of the vehicle, was treated for his injuries and taken to a local hospital. He was later arrested and charged with operating under the influence.

The crash remains under investigation by Marblehead police and state police. The district attorney’s office said no further details were immediately available since both parties are juveniles.

Anyone with information about the crash is being urged to call Marblehead Police Detective Sgt. Sean Brady at 781-631-1212.

news_communities.title: Neighbors leaving their garbage at Malden High School amid ongoing trash strike
news_communities.author: Munashe Kwangwari
news_communities.summary:
As the school year inches closer, high schoolers in Malden, Massachusetts could be walking into a smelly situation as neighbors drop off garbage bags amid the ongoing Republic Services trash strike.

Dumpsters at Malden High School have been overflowing, and the same thing is going on at other schools on the North Shore.

This is the result of the ongoing labor dispute between Republic and their striking employees, represented by Teamsters Local 25. The union members are seeking more money and better benefits that they say would put them in line with the competition.

The strike began on July 1, but it appears no end is in sight. The last time the two sides met was on July 18.

One city councilor in Malden says the trash has been piling up at the high school for so long, that the number of rats in the area has increased, leading to a public health hazard.

Governor Maura Healey and now Senator Ed Markey are specifically calling out Republic, saying the company needs to do more to try to end this situation. Meanwhile, schools starts next week in Malden.

news_communities.title: Man arrested for attacking person with hammer outside East Boston bakery
news_communities.author: Marc Fortier
news_communities.summary:
A man is charged with attacking a person with a hammer outside a popular East Boston bakery earlier this week.

Eric Vargas, 37, of Boston, was arrested Wednesday and charged with one count of assault and battery with a dangerous weapon.

The arrest stemmed from an incident that occurred on Tuesday morning.

Boston police said a victim entered their station around 11:30 a.m. Tuesday and said that he had been attacked by a man with a hammer earlier that morning. The victim told police that he was entering La Sultana Bakery at 40 Maverick Square around 6:30 a.m. when an unknown man approached and asked to use his cell phone.

The victim told the man that he didn’t have a cellphone, and proceeded into the bakery. When he left and began walking toward his vehicle, the suspect allegedly hit him in the head with a hammer.

The victim sustained serious, but non-life-threatening injuries and was treated at a nearby health center.

Detectives launched an investigation and were able to obtain and disseminate images of the suspect, which led them to identify him as Vargas.

Shortly after 5 p.m. Wednesday, patrol officers located Vargas sitting on the steps of a building on Paris Street in East Boston and placed him under arrest. He was expected to be arraigned Thursday afternoon in East Boston District Court.

news_communities.title: Nurse accused of faking records to steal pills from East Boston rehab center
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A nurse from Cape Cod is accused of falsifying medical records at an East Boston rehab facility to steal oxycodone pills, the Suffolk County District Attorney’s Office said Monday.

Kaitlin Tipton, 34, of West Yarmouth, was charged with drug larceny and drug obtained by fraud. Tipton, according to the DA’s office, is a licensed practical nurse.

Prosecutors allege that Tipton falsified drug records and other medical records to divert more than 50 of the highly addictive pills from Advocate Health Care (AHC) – where she worked – from November 2023 through February 2024. Tipton is accused of not documenting doses, removing more medication than ordered, and making false entries on dates when she was not working.

Tipton told investigators that she stole the pills for a friend with back problems, the DA said.

“We’ll likely never know where these pills ended up, but we do know that substances like oxycodone and oxycontin are huge contributors to dependency issues we see in Boston and every other community. So diversions from dispensing facilities must be taken seriously,” District Attorney Kevin Hayden said in a media release.

Tipton was released on personal recognizance and ordered to stay away from AHC. She is scheduled for a probable cause hearing on Sept. 10.

news_communities.title: North Shore mayors team up in bid to end strike as trash piles up
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino, NBC10 Boston Staff Reports and Malcolm Johnson
news_communities.summary:
Several communities impacted by a weekslong trash strike are taking legal action as waste continues to pile up.

Waste collection workers from Republic Services have been on strike since July 1 over contract negotiations. Teamsters Local 25, the union representing the workers, is calling for higher wages, a different health plan. Despite the involvement of a federal mediator, the sides have failed to come to an agreement.

Local 25 said Thursday that dozens of nonmembers who work at Republic Services had asked to join the union, and for representatives to negotiate on their behalf.

“What is amazing is that we never sought them out. They came to us and asked how they can join Local 25,” the union’s president, Tom Mari, said in a statement. “Contrary to the lies constantly being told by Republic to us at the bargaining table, and to their own non-union employees, these employees are telling us their health insurance is not free and they pay more than $800 per month for the company’s lousy health insurance and their wages and working conditions suck. When I told them that companies that sign contracts with us provide health insurance at no cost, they immediately wanted to sign up.”

Beverly Mayor Mike Cahill announced Wednesday that his city and five others intended to file a complaint in Salem Superior Court over what he described as public health problems caused by the lack of trash collection.

Town administrations and mayors from Peabody, Beverly, Malden, Gloucester, Danvers and Canton joined Cahill on the complaint.

“It’s like a 100-degree blizzard out here dealing with this for the last 17 days,” said Bob Knox, Malden’s Department of Public Works director.

The issues are causing tensions and in some cases, turning dangerous.

“One of our residents who did miss dropping off trash during last night’s drop off time, threatened to shoot one of our employees this morning,” explained Malden Mayor Gary Christenson.

Beverly also intends to withhold part of its next scheduled payment based off the costs they’ve incurred by the issue, Cahill said.

“We’re not getting consistent information. Republic will say we’re going to pick up all trash and recycling on Monday, so we go to the public and tell them to put the trash and recycling out and then they don’t show up so it really makes us look like we don’t know what’s going on….but that’s because we don’t know what’s going on because we’re getting mixed signals,” Gloucester Mayor Greg Verga said.

Republic Services was issued a summons to appear in Salem Superior Court on Monday at 2 p.m. NBC10 Boston has reached out to the company requesting comment on the complaint.

A press release from the union stated that at the end of the session, the federal mediator reported that the company refused to agree to any future negotiating sessions. The company disputed that in a statement later Wednesday.

“At the end of negotiations Tuesday, we offered to meet on Friday to continue our discussions. Teamsters officials refused to meet,” Republic Services said. “Today, the mediator notified us that they changed their minds and are now willing to meet on Friday. We hope the Teamsters come prepared to reach an agreement that’s good for everyone.”

More than a dozen cities and towns have been impacted by the strike, which has now entered its third week. State officials have weighed in, the latest being Gov. Maura Healey, who called on Republic to reach a deal in a statement issued Thursday.

“It has now been three weeks of no trash pick up in several Massachusetts communities, and this has gone beyond a headache for residents, businesses and municipalities – it is a public health concern and it’s expensive for everyone. Sanitation workers do essential work to keep our neighborhoods clean and healthy, and they deserve fair wages, benefits and protections,” the governor said. “Republic Services needs to come to the table and reach a fair deal – it’s time to get people back to work and resume services to our communities as soon as possible.” 

As the strike continues, at least two towns are threatening to cut ties with Republic Services. Last week, attorneys for Lynnfield and North Reading sent non-performance notices to the company.

Other towns are getting creative as residents deal with the impact. In Canton, for a $20 donation, Canton Little League will pick up trash and bring it to the dump.

More on this story

No end to Mass. trash strike after new negotiations, more officials weigh in

‘Deeply concerned': Boston City Councilors push for end to trash strike

news_communities.title: As trash piles up during strike, Peabody calls for help from Bill Gates
news_communities.author: Brianna Borghi
news_communities.summary:
A trash strike has been in effect more than two weeks in Massachusetts communities, and one is reaching out to a billionaire for help.

After a 12-hour negotiating session on Tuesday, Republic Services and Teamsters Local 25 are no closer to reaching a deal to end the strike that has been going on since July 1.

A press release from the union stated that at the end of the session, the federal mediator reported that the company refused to agree to any future negotiating sessions. The company disputed that in a statement later Wednesday.

“At the end of negotiations Tuesday, we offered to meet on Friday to continue our discussions. Teamsters officials refused to meet,” Republic Services said. “Today, the mediator notified us that they changed their minds and are now willing to meet on Friday. We hope the Teamsters come prepared to reach an agreement that’s good for everyone.”

More than a dozen cities and towns have been impacted by the strike, which has now entered its third week. The Peabody City Council is taking further action by calling upon Bill Gates to step in.

“We respectfully urge you to use your influence as the principal of Cascade Investment, LLC, a major shareholder of Republic Services, to take immediate and constructive action,” the council wrote in a letter.

“I don’t even know if he knows what’s going on. He’s pro-planet, gives billions of dollars to certain issues, and this is a big issue,” said City Councilor David Gamache.

The letter asks Gates to urge Republic Services leadership to return to the negotiating table in good faith; encourage a swift and fair resolution that respects workers’ contributions and addresses their legitimate demands; and affirm his commitment to responsible corporate governance.

“I am very optimistic that once the news gets out to him, that he will finally put his foot down and say, ‘Let’s negotiate,'” Gamache said.

As the strike continues, at least two towns are threatening to cut ties with Republic Services. Last week, attorneys for Lynnfield and North Reading sent non-performance notices to the company.

Other towns are getting creative as residents deal with the impact. In Canton, for a $20 donation, Canton Little League will pick up trash and bring it to the dump.

news_communities.title: 7 Chinese nationals face federal charges in multi-million-dollar marijuana grow operation
news_communities.author: Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
Federal prosecutors in Massachusetts said Tuesday that seven Chinese nationals are facing charges in an alleged marijuana grow operation that spanned multiple states and yielded millions of dollars.

The office of U.S. Attorney Leah Foley announced the arrests of six people alongside the Federal Bureau of Investigation and Massachusetts State Police.

Investigators alleged that Chinese nationals were smuggled into the country to work in a network of grow houses concealed in single-family homes in Massachusetts and Maine — and that their passports were withheld “until they repaid their smuggling debts.”

The group allegedly ran the grow houses since around January of 2020, operating in Braintree, Melrose and Greenfield, “among other locations in Massachusetts, Maine and elsewhere,” according to Foley’s office.

Defendants are accused of conspiring to sell kilograms of marijuana in bulk across the Northeast, including cash transactions with operators in New York.

Those arrested Tuesday morning include 39-year-old Jianxiong Chen of Braintree, 36-year-old Yuxiong Wu of Weymouth, 38-year-old Dinghui Li of Braintree, 35-year-old Dechao Ma of Braintree, 35-year-old Peng Lian Zhu of Melrose and 35-year-old Hongbin Wu of Quincy. Yanrong Zhu, 47, of Greenfield, who also resides in Brooklyn, New York, has not been apprehended.

All seven people have been indicted on a count of conspiracy to manufacture, distribute and possess with intent to distribute marijuana.

Chen, Yuxiong Wu, Li, Ma and Peng Lian Zhu are also each charged with one count of money laundering conspiracy. Additionally, Chen faces 11 counts of money laundering and one count of bringing aliens into the United States. Yuxiong Wu is charged with four counts of money laundering, while Li and Ma each face two counts of money laundering.

“This case pulls back the curtain on a sprawling criminal enterprise that exploited our immigration system and our communities for personal gain,” Foley said in a statement. “These defendants allegedly turned quiet homes across the Northeast into hubs for a criminal enterprise – building a multi-million-dollar black-market operation off the backs of an illegal workforce and using our neighborhoods as cover. That ends today.”

Chen allegedly controlled multiple grow houses in Maine and a Braintree home that authorities said served as a base of the group’s operations. Others in the group allegedly brought marijuana and cash to the Braintree home to be redistributed, concealing it in their vehicles’ engine compartments.

Prosecutors said the home was searched in October of 2024, with investigators finding over $270,000 in cash inside the house and in a Porsche in the driveway. Several Chinese passports and other identifying documents were allegedly found in a safe, as well.

Chen is accused of putting people to work at the grow houses “while keeping possession of their passports until they repaid him for the cost associated with smuggling them into the country,” Foley’s office wrote.

Ma’s home in Braintree and Peng Lian Zhu’s home in Melrose were also searched in October, authorities said, with over 109 kilograms in marijuana being seized, as well as almost $200,000 in cash and other luxury items, including a gold Rolex watch with a price tag reading “$65,000” still on it.

In June of 2023, Hongbin Wu and Yanrong Zhu were stopped by law enforcement, allegedly after leaving a grow house in Greenfield. Authorities said $36,900 in cash was seized.

The group allegedly made millions of dollars in profit from the marijuana sales, using it to buy luxury homes, vehicles, jewelry and other items. The money also funded the expansion of the enterprise, authorities said.

The marijuana manufacturing and distribution conspiracy charge all of the defendants are facing carries a sentence of up to five years in prison, at least two years of supervised release and a fine of up to $250,000, Foley’s office said. A charge of money laundering conspiracy provides for a sentence of up to 20 years in prison, up to three years of supervised release and a fine of up to $500,000, or twice the amount involved in the case. Each count of money laundering carries a sentence of up to 10 years in prison, up to three years of supervised release and a fine of up to $500,000, or twice the amount involved in the case. For the charge of bringing aliens into the United States, Chen faces three to 10 years in prison, three years of supervised release and a fine as high as $250,000.

It was not immediately clear if the defendants had attorneys who could answer to the charges against them.

Federal prosecutors said that in addition to the FBI and Massachusetts State Police, the Drug Enforcement Agency provided assistance, as well as New York State Police, Maine State Police, and local authorities in Braintree, New York City and neighboring Westchester County.

news_communities.title: Trash, recycling workers go on strike in numerous Mass. towns
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
Hundreds of sanitation workers went on strike early Tuesday in the Boston area, their union announced, an action that was set to affect waste collection in over a dozen communities.

Teamsters Local 25 said 450 workers who are represented by the union started a strike just after midnight, which will affect hundreds of thousands of people in the area amid ongoing contract negotiations with their employer, Republic Services.

The union said its members are demanding better wages, benefits and labor protections in their contract, but the national company won’t budge. NBC10 Boston has reached out to Republic Services for comment.

The strike affects these towns, according to the union: Peabody, Manchester-by-the-Sea, Gloucester, Wakefield, Marblehead, Malden, Topsfield, Saugus, Beverly, Danvers, North Reading, Lynnfield, Revere, Swampscott, Arlington, Watertown and Canton. But Republic said Arlington, Topsfield and Saugus are not impacted by the strike.

The union noted that other local union groups representing Republic workers across the country could call similar strikes as well.

“If your rubbish is piling up on the Fourth of July, remember who’s responsible for it: the white-collar criminals who run Republic Services,” said Teamsters General President Sean O’Brien in a statement. “Republic Teamsters didn’t start this fight, but we will finish it. Our members will do whatever it takes to finally get the respect they’re owed.”

The town of North Reading urged its residents not to put out trash or recycling on Tuesday, due to the Republic Services strike. The town said it’s been told of contingency staffing plans in the event of a strike, and would share more information Tuesday.

Canton issued a similar notice for Tuesday.

“We understand the frustration this sudden disruption may cause and appreciate residents’ patience as we work closely with Republic Services to restore collection services as quickly and safely as possible,” Town Administrator Charles Doody said in a statement.

The union initially said on social media that 20 towns were affected.

news_communities.title: Mass. city leaders call on U.S. attorney to investigate local ICE actions
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
A group of leaders of over a dozen Massachusetts cities issued a statement regarding recent ICE actions Friday, highlighting the level of anxiety being created in immigrant communities and requesting that the U.S. Attorney’s Office investigate these practices.

“For the past several weeks, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officials have been entering some of our communities and arresting residents. ICE has stated that they are prioritizing the removal of people who pose a threat to public safety. However, increasingly, ICE is arresting bystanders with no criminal record and removing so-called ‘collaterals’ without regard to the impact on our communities,” the statement reads.

The letter was sent jointly by Boston Mayor Michelle Wu, Brookline Town Administrator Chas Carey, Cambridge City Manager Yi-An Huang, Chelsea City Manager Fidel Maltez, Everett Mayor Carlo DeMaria, Lynn Mayor Jared Nicholson, Malden Mayor Gary Christenson, Medford Mayor Breanna Lungo-Koehn, Melrose Mayor Jen Grigoraitis, Newton Mayor Ruthanne Fuller, Revere Mayor Patrick Keefe, Salem Mayor Dominick Pangallo, Somerville Mayor Katjana Ballantyne and Worcester Mayor Joseph Petty.

The letter said that immigrants are vital members of their communities, contributing to their vitality, but have been reporting, they said:

  • ICE agents who have detained residents because they appear to be someone who could or “should” be detained. They allege that this raises questions about whether ICE is racially profiling.
  • Occasions where ICE detains individuals and then sends them to another jurisdiction without providing any information.
  • Cases of ICE agents breaking into vehicles without a warrant.

“Our residents are impacted deeply by these actions. Families are frightened to leave their homes to go to school, medical appointments, workplaces, or church. These actions are fomenting distrust between residents and local law enforcement officials, which further erodes public safety and destabilizes our communities. These actions and some accompanying statements conflating all immigrants with violent criminals stigmatize an entire group of people based on ethnicity, race, or origin, putting them and others at increasing risk of harm or possibly the violation of their rights,” the statement said.

The group of officials stated that the actions are “jeopardizing public safety” and called on federal prosecutors to investigate the practices over potential constitutional violations. They also requested that ICE make public its policies on “how individuals are targeted, due process, and the use of force.”

“We know that these ICE actions are spurring fear and anger, which is increasing the number of demonstrations and protests in our communities. We call on everyone in the Commonwealth to peacefully exercise their freedom of speech and to respect the role of law enforcement in maintaining public order,” the letter concluded.

The letter was sent amid a spat between Wu and U.S. Attorney for Massachusetts Leah Foley over immigration enforcement. After Wu told WBUR that people are “getting snatched off the street by secret police,” Foley called her out in a video statement, saying Wu and others “have created false narratives” over the work of arresting undocumented immigrants.

“We will not apologize for doing our job,” Foley said in the statement.

But Wu stood by her remarks, saying, “I’m just describing what Bostonians have seen with their own eyes.”

On Sunday, acting ICE Director Todd Lyons released a statement referring to Wu and Massachusetts Gov. Healey and standing by ICE agents:

Politicians like Mayor Wu and Governor Healey have loudly touted lawlessness over law and order. Their prioritization of criminal aliens in communities across the state should speak volumes to their constituents. As these elected officials welcome those flouting national laws, ICE’s brave law enforcement officers protecting public safety will continue to perform the job they refuse to do.
